message.content:
Research Unix/Linux RC file systems and dotfile conventions.

Focus on:
1. Most sophisticated RC systems:
   - .bashrc, .zshrc (shell)
   - .npmrc, .yarnrc
   - .eslintrc, .prettierrc
   - .gitconfig
   - .editorconfig

2. For each, document:
   - File naming convention
   - Import/extend/inherit mechanisms
   - Cascade behavior (global → user → project → local)
   - File format (JSON, YAML, INI, custom)

3. Which has the most sophisticated features?
   - Imports from other files
   - Extends/inheritance
   - Override patterns
   - Environment variable expansion

4. Historical origin of "rc" (run commands)

Return a structured comparison of RC systems and their sophistication levels.

Here are the search results for ESLint configuration cascading and extends inheritance:

## ESLint Configuration: Extends and Cascading

### Modern Flat Config (Current)

A configuration object uses extends to inherit all the traits of another configuration object or array (including rules, plugins, and language options) and can then modify all the options.(no content)The extends key is an array of values indicating which configurations to extend from. The elements of the extends array can be one of three values: a string that specifies the name of a configuration in a plugin... ESLint plugins can export predefined configurations.

**When to use `extends`:**
- For reusing configurations - When you want to inherit and build upon existing configurations from plugins, shareable packages, or predefined configs. For plugin configuration - When applying recommended or specific configurations from ESLint plugins.
- For predefined configurations - When using ESLint's built-in configurations like js/recommended or js/all. For modular configuration - When you want to compose multiple configuration sources into a single configuration object.

**When to use Cascading:**
- For file-specific rules - When you need different rules for different file patterns or directories.(no content)This page explains how to use flat config files. For the deprecated eslintrc format, see the deprecated documentation.

### Deprecated .eslintrc Configuration Cascading

A configuration file, once extended, can inherit all the traits of another configuration file (including rules, plugins, and language options) and modify all the options.(no content)When using .eslintrc.* and package.json files for configuration, you can take advantage of configuration cascading. The configuration cascade works based on the location of the file being linted. If there is an .eslintrc file in the same directory as the file being linted, then that configuration takes precedence. ESLint then searches up the directory structure, merging any .eslintrc files it finds along the way until reaching either an .eslintrc file with root: true or the root directory.(no content)ESLint continues searching for .eslintrc.* and package.json files in ancestor directories up to and including the root directory or until a config with "root": true is found.

### Configuration Hierarchy

The configuration cascade works by using the closest .eslintrc file to the file being linted as the highest priority, then any configuration files in the parent directory, and so on.(no content)Glob pattern overrides have higher precedence than the regular configuration in the same config file. Multiple overrides within the same config are applied in order. That is, the last override block in a config file always has the highest precedence.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about npmrc and yarnrc configuration cascade (global, user, project, local):

## .npmrc Configuration Cascade

The `.npmrc` file can exist in two main locations:
- **Project-level**: `./.npmrc` (in your project root) - applies only to that project
- **Global**: `~/.npmrc` (user home directory) - applies to all npm commands run by the user(no content)The interesting part about this is the overriding behavior of the deeper configs. If you are currently in `~/projects/cool_app/`, the `.npmrc` file in this directory will take priority over the one in `~`.(no content)When you run `yarn install` it searches for `.npmrc` and `.yarnrc` from your directory all the way up to your `~`. You can see this happen by running `yarn install --verbose`.

## .yarnrc Configuration (Yarn 1 Classic)

Yarn 1's `.yarnrc` is similar to `.npmrc` but uses slightly different prefixes for registry URLs. It supports project-level (`./.yarnrc`) and global (`~/.yarnrc`) files.(no content)By running yarn, it composes all of the configuration values from `.npmrc` and `.yarnrc` into a single global dictionary which you can verify by running `yarn config list`.

## .yarnrc.yml Configuration (Yarn 2+ Berry)

The global config is saved in `~/.yarnrc.yml` (Yarn 2.0 onwards, previously it was called `.yarnrc`). If you set a config flag in a directory where there is no `.yarnrc.yml`, it'll add the config to the global config file at `~/.yarnrc.yml`.(no content)While `.npmrc` uses a simple INI format, Yarn 1 and Yarn 2+ have distinct config structures—Yarn 1 uses `.yarnrc` (INI-style), and Yarn 2+ uses `.yarnrc.yml` (YAML with `npmRegistries`/`npmScopes`).(no content)Yarn modern does not recognize `--registry` or `.npmrc` file anymore. For defining a registry you must use the `.yarnrc.yml` located in the root of your project or global configuration.

## Configuration Lookup Order

When running yarn (Classic/v1), it checks multiple locations as shown in verbose output:
The configuration file search order includes checking the project directory first, then parent directories up to the home directory, and also system locations like `/usr/local/etc/npmrc`.

Based on the search results, here's information about the EditorConfig inheritance and override mechanism:

## EditorConfig Inheritance and Override Mechanism

### Hierarchical Application
Properties from matching EditorConfig sections are applied in the order they were read, so properties in closer files take precedence. This means that properties found in an .editorconfig file closer to the file will override ones found further away from the file.(no content)EditorConfig files apply settings hierarchically, affecting files in their directory and all subdirectories. You can override these settings at any level by placing a new .editorconfig file in a subdirectory.

### The `root=true` Property
By setting `root=true` in the .editorconfig file at a specific level, you can prevent the inheritance of settings from parent directories. This feature is particularly useful when you do not have control over the files in higher-level directories.

### How Overriding Works
To override some or all of the EditorConfig settings, add an .editorconfig file at the level of the file hierarchy you want those overridden settings to apply. The new EditorConfig file settings apply to files at the same level and files in any subdirectories. If you want to override some, but not all of the settings, specify just those settings in the .editorconfig file. Only those properties that you explicitly list in the lower-level .editorconfig file are overridden.

### File Search Behavior
When opening a file, EditorConfig plugins look for a file named .editorconfig (all lowercase) in the directory of the opened file and in every parent directory.

### Global vs Local Configuration
Local .editorconfig files should have `root = false` defined so that presentation-only (and other) properties can be inherited from the global .editorconfig file.(no content)Any properties defined in your repository's local .editorconfig file will override the global ones.

### IDE-Specific Behavior
Editor settings in Visual Studio are only respected while editing and aren't respected at build time or by other IDEs. Additionally, if the project or solution opened inside Visual Studio has an EditorConfig file, then options from the EditorConfig file take precedence.(no content)Concerning JetBrains Rider, by default the IDE adheres to .editorconfig settings, which will supersede any preferences set within JetBrains Rider's own settings.

Linux Configuration Files Explained — linuxvox.com","url":"https://linuxvox.com/blog/what-does-rc-mean-in-dot-files/"},{"title":"What Are Dotfiles? | Richie Codes","url":"https://www.richie.codes/blog/what-are-dotfiles/"},{"title":"Meaning of RC in a Dotfile - Today I Learned","url":"https://til.hashrocket.com/posts/zf2nzhqnsx-meaning-of-rc-in-a-dotfile"},{"title":"The Meaning of 'rc' in Dot Files - Javascript","url":"https://copyprogramming.com/howto/what-does-rc-mean-in-dot-files"},{"title":"What Is rc and What Are rc Files? | Baeldung on Linux","url":"https://www.baeldung.com/linux/rc-files"},{"title":"why we have rc word in config file names","url":"https://www.softpost.org/programming/why-we-have-rc-word-in-config-file-names"},{"title":"Guide to Unix/Files - Wikibooks, open books for an open world","url":"https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Guide_to_Unix/Files"}]

Based on my search, here's what I found about the history and origin of Unix RC files and the dotfiles convention:

## Origin of "RC" in Unix Files

The 'rc' suffix is ubiquitous in dot files, but its origin is surprisingly debated. The most widely accepted explanation traces 'rc' to "Run Commands", a term from early computing history.

### The CTSS Connection

In the 1960s, MIT developed the Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS)—one of the first time-sharing operating systems. CTSS included a feature called "runcom" (short for "run commands"), which executed a script of commands stored in a file. Users could create RUNCOM files to automate tasks, and this concept later influenced Unix.(no content)The term is thought to have originated in 1965 from the runcom facility in the MIT Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS).

### Adoption in Unix

When Unix was developed in the 1970s, its creators (Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie) borrowed the "run commands" idea. The system's startup script was named /etc/rc (still used today in some Linux distros), short for "run commands." Over time, this convention trickled down to user-specific configuration files like .bashrc and .vimrc.(no content)There existed a facility named "runcom" that would execute a set of commands stored in a file. This file was commonly referred to as "a runcom," and the abbreviation "rc" in Unix originated from this usage.

### Alternative Interpretation

The rc in .zshrc stands for "run commands" or "run control", depending on who you ask.(no content)The "rc" suffix originally stood for "run control," indicating that these files controlled how the shell or other programs would run or behave during startup.

## The Dotfiles Convention

In UNIX, there's a convention whereby the names of files and folders which are meant to be hidden from view by default are prefixed with a dot. Unsurprisingly, these are called "dotfiles" or "dot folders". When they say "hidden", they mean hidden from the ls command.(no content)Dot files (or "hidden files") are configuration files in Unix-like systems whose names start with a dot (.). This naming convention hides them by default from standard ls commands (you'll need ls -a to list them). They live in your home directory (~) and control the behavior of applications, shells, and system tools.

### Common RC Files

Over time, the "rc" naming convention has been adopted for various configuration files beyond shell scripts, such as .vimrc for configuring the Vim text editor or .bashrc for configuring the Bash shell environment. .bashrc is the configuration file for the Bash shell (bash), containing shell commands that are executed when a Bash session starts.

## Cosmiconfig Overview

Cosmiconfig is a flexible configuration loader for JavaScript projects. It searches for and loads configuration from various sources, including package.json, JSON or YAML files with extensions like .json, .yaml, .yml, .js, or .cjs, and other custom locations.

## Key Features

Cosmiconfig looks for configuration in several places: in a package.json property, an rc file, a .config.js file, and rc files with extensions. It has built-in support for JSON, YAML, and CommonJS formats.(no content)It stops at the first configuration found, instead of finding all that can be found up the directory tree and merging them automatically. It's asynchronous by default (though can be run synchronously).

## Extends and Merge Functionality

Cosmiconfig offers advanced features like config merging and extensibility, making it suitable for complex applications. It's ideal for projects that need to discover and load external configuration files.

### Merging Loaders
If you provide a loaders object, your object will be merged with the defaults.(no content)So you can override one or two without having to override them all.

### mergeSearchPlaces Option
You can configure cosmiconfig with options like `mergeSearchPlaces: false` to enforce a custom naming convention and format without merging with the tool-defined search places (`true` is the default setting).

### Extending Configurations
From the code examples, you can implement extends functionality by:
Using an `extends` property in options, then loading and merging each shareable config: "If `extends` is defined, load and merge each shareable config with `options`".

### Options Interface
The CommonOptions interface includes properties like `mergeImportArrays: boolean` and `mergeSearchPlaces: boolean`.

## Custom Loaders

Custom loaders allow configuration syntaxes that aren't handled by Cosmiconfig's defaults, like JSON5, INI, or XML. They also allow ES2015 modules from .mjs configuration files, and parsing JS files with Babel before deriving the configuration.(no content)Custom loader functions have the signature `(filepath: string, content: string) => Object | null` for sync, or returning a Promise for async. Based on the search results, here's what I found about Prettier configuration, extends, inheritance, and shareable configs:

## Key Findings

### Prettier Does NOT Support Native "extends" Mechanism

Prettier does not offer an "extends" mechanism as you might be familiar from tools such as ESLint.(no content)However it's lacking support for extending shared configurations.

### How Shareable Configs Work

Sharable configs are just npm packages that export a single prettier config file.

**Simple Usage (no customization):**
You can reference a shared config directly in your `package.json`:
```json
{
  "prettier": "@username/prettier-config"
}
```

Or in a `.prettierrc.json` file, just use the package name as a string.

### How to Extend/Override a Shared Config

To extend the configuration to overwrite some properties from the shared configuration, import the file in a .prettierrc.mjs file and export the modifications.

Example from the official docs:
```javascript
import usernamePrettierConfig from "@username/prettier-config";

const config = {
  ...usernamePrettierConfig,
  semi: false,
};

export default config;
```

To extend a configuration you will need to: Import/Require this sharable config from within your own configuration. Extend your modification on top of the shared config using something like Object destructuring, Object.assign(), or lodash.merge().

### Configuration File Support

Prettier uses cosmiconfig for configuration file support. This means you can configure prettier via: A .prettierrc file, written in YAML or JSON, with optional extensions: .yaml/.yml/.json. A .prettierrc.toml file, written in TOML (the .toml extension is required). A prettier.config.js or .prettierrc.js file that exports an object. A "prettier" key in your package.json file.

### Important Limitation

This method does not offer a way to extend the configuration to overwrite some properties from the shared configuration. If you need to override properties, you must use a JavaScript config file (`.prettierrc.js` or `.prettierrc.mjs`).

Here's information about Git's `include` and `includeIf` conditional configuration features:

## Git Configuration Include and Conditional Includes

### Basic `[include]` Directive

The `include` and `includeIf` sections work the same, except the `includeIf` has a condition that must be satisfied. They let you insert configuration from another file into your main one.

### The `[includeIf]` Directive

Git has a wonderful directive called `[includeIf]`. Basically with this directive you can import an additional configuration file based on a condition being true.

### How It Works

You can conditionally include a config file from another by setting an `includeIf.<condition>.path` variable to the name of the file to be included. The condition starts with a keyword followed by a colon and some data whose format and meaning depends on the keyword.

### The `gitdir` Keyword

The data that follows the keyword `gitdir` and a colon is used as a glob pattern. If the location of the .git directory matches the pattern, the include condition is met.(no content)The pattern can contain standard globbing wildcards and two additional ones, `**/` and `/**`, that can match multiple path components.

### Common Use Case: Managing Multiple Identities

A common use case is wanting to create work commits with a work email and vice versa for open source commits. Thanks to conditional includes, you can dynamically switch instances of your .gitconfig on the fly.

### Example Configuration

With conditional configuration includes, you have a lot more control over how your Git configuration is applied. You can set up some configuration to be applied based on the directory that you're in, so it's much easier to set up.

Example from a `.gitconfig`:
```ini
[user]
    name = Your Name
    email = default@email.com

[includeIf "gitdir:~/work/"]
    path = ~/.gitconfig-work

[includeIf "gitdir:~/personal/"]
    path = ~/.gitconfig-personal
```

For example, due to `[includeIf "gitdir:/tmp/jdsalaro/gitlab/**"]` any repository which we clone inside of `/tmp/jdsalaro/gitlab/` will be configured according to the global and local .gitconfig files as well as the specified path. Of course, since the user's identity is defined both globally and in the conditionally included Git configuration the former's user section will be overridden by the latter.

### Case-Insensitive Matching

You can use `gitdir/i:` for case-insensitive matching:
```ini
[includeIf "gitdir/i:~/source/work/"]
    path = "~/.config/git/work-config"
```

### Remote URL-based Includes

If there exists at least one remote URL that matches a pattern, the include condition is met. Files included by this option (directly or indirectly) are not allowed to contain remote URLs.

### Path Resolution

The `includeIf` operates on (partial) paths, so the include works for every repository that was cloned inside the directories specified. The path parts are relative to the file that includes them (like `~/.gitconfig`), rather than relative to the repository they operate on.

Here's what I found about bashrc/zshrc source, import, and include mechanisms for configuration:

## Overview of Shell Configuration Files

The .bashrc or .zshrc file serves as a central configuration file for customizing the behavior of the Bash and Zsh shell respectively, making it a powerful tool for enhancing productivity.(no content).bashrc is the configuration file for the Bash shell (Bourne Again Shell), while .zshrc serves the same purpose for the Zsh shell (Z Shell). These files are loaded every time you open a new terminal session, applying your custom settings automatically.

## The Source Mechanism

When you make changes to that configuration file, those changes would not reflect in your current terminal session. Remember that file only ran at the beginning. The new changes (for example, declared variables) would only reflect in a "new" terminal session. So you would have to close the current session, then create a new one.(no content)You don't always need to source configuration files. The terminal already does that at the beginning of a session. If you want access to the updated configuration, you can create a new session. But, if you want access to the updated configuration in the same terminal session, then source is a friendly tool for that purpose.

## Loading Order

The issue is that Bash sources from a different file based on what kind of shell it thinks it is in. For an "interactive non-login shell", it reads .bashrc, but for an "interactive login shell" it reads from the first of .bash_profile, .bash_login and .profile (only).(no content)This means putting most of your stuff in ~/.bashrc, having exactly one of ~/.bash_profile, ~/.bash_login, and ~/.profile, and sourcing ~/.bashrc from it.

## Splitting Configuration into Multiple Files

There's no observable difference in performance between sourcing from a single .zshrc/.bashrc or sourcing from its split files. Splitting the files will mean better modularity, separation of concerns, and scalability. Future updates will be easier and more manageable.(no content)You can keep aliases and functions in a separate file so that it doesn't clutter zshrc. You can try to do the same: Copy your custom alias and functions from ~/.zshrc, paste these commands in a file called ~/.custom-alias, and put this line at the end of your .zshrc: `source ~/.custom-alias`.

An example pattern for loading environment-specific configurations:
You can create environment-specific configurations in ~/.zshrc such as loading work-specific configuration with `if [[ -f "$HOME/.zshrc.work" ]]; then source "$HOME/.zshrc.work" fi` and loading local machine-specific configuration similarly.

## Zsh Startup Files

Zsh introduces 5 startup files, each one sourced depending on the type of shell invocation. The two most important are .zshrc and .zshenv which works like this: .zshenv is sourced on all invocations of the shell. It often contains exported variables that should be available to other programs, for example, $PATH. .zshrc is sourced in interactive shells only. It should contain commands to set up aliases, functions, options, key bindings, etc.

Here's what I found about TypeScript tsconfig.json extends inheritance and compilerOptions:

## How `extends` Works in tsconfig.json

"The value of extends is a string which contains a path to another configuration file to inherit from. The path may use Node.js style resolution. The configuration from the base file are loaded first, then overridden by those in the inheriting config file."

### Key Inheritance Rules

**compilerOptions are merged:**
When you extend a tsconfig.json, the properties in compilerOptions are merged. And when both files define the same property, the child tsconfig.json wins.

**files, include, exclude are replaced (not merged):**
It's worth noting that files, include, and exclude from the inheriting config file overwrite those from the base config file, and that circularity between configuration files is not allowed.

**references are excluded from inheritance:**
Currently, the only top-level property that is excluded from inheritance is references.

### Path Resolution

All relative paths found in the configuration file will be resolved relative to the configuration file they originated in.(no content)The problem is the paths in a tsconfig.json are relative to the folder that that specific tsconfig.json file is in, and not the tsconfig.json doing the extending.(no content)That means we always have to define baseUrl, rootDir, outDir, include, and exclude in the child tsconfig.json (the one that extends the base tsconfig.json).

### Using Shared Base Configs

Depending on the JavaScript runtime environment which you intend to run your code in, there may be a base configuration which you can use at github.com/tsconfig/bases. These are tsconfig.json files which your project extends from which simplifies your tsconfig.json by handling the runtime support.(no content)TypeScript will climb up node_modules folders looking for a package. For each of those packages, TypeScript will first check whether package.json contains a "tsconfig" field, and if it does, TypeScript will try to load a configuration file from that field.

### Best Practice for Multiple Environments

Use a base config and extend it in each environment: tsconfig.base.json, tsconfig.json for dev, tsconfig.build.json for production build, tsconfig.test.json for tests, and tsconfig.lint.json for ESLint if needed.

Here's what I found about Babel configuration, inheritance, and the `extends` option:

## Configuration Priority

The configuration priority order is: `babel.config.json` < `.babelrc` < programmatic options. In other words, `babel.config.json` is overwritten by `.babelrc`, and `.babelrc` is overwritten by programmatic options.

## Using `extends` for Configuration Inheritance

One alternative for monorepo structures would be to create a `.babelrc` in each sub-package that uses "extends" to inherit from a base configuration. Unfortunately, this approach can be a bit repetitive, and depending on how Babel is being used, could require setting "babelrcRoots".

## Configuration File Types

- `babel.config.js` and `.babelrc.js` behave like the `.mjs` equivalents when your `package.json` file contains the `"type": "module"` option, otherwise they are exactly the same as the `.cjs` files.
- For compatibility reasons, `.babelrc` is an alias for `.babelrc.json`.
- For more complex configurations a `babel.config.js` file in the project's root is used instead. Since it's a JavaScript file, it's much more flexible than `.babelrc`.

## Configuration Merging Behavior

Babel's configuration merging is relatively straightforward. Options will overwrite existing options when they are present and their value is not undefined. There are, however, a few special cases: For `assumptions`, `parserOpts` and `generatorOpts`, objects are merged, rather than replaced. For plugins and presets, they are replaced based on the identity of the plugin/preset object/function itself combined with the name of the entry.

## Preset Ordering

Preset ordering is reversed (last to first). For example, presets listed as `[a, b, c]` will run in the following order: c, b, then a.(no content)Plugins run before Presets. The Plugin order is first to last. The Preset order is last to first instead.

## Potential Issues with `extends`

There can be ordering problems between the extended and the main config - the question of whether extended config elements should have precedence over the main config has been raised as an issue.

## Using with Webpack's babel-loader

The options passed to babel-loader will be merged with Babel config files, e.g., `babel.config.js` or `.babelrc`.

---

# Sistemas RC Unix/Linux e Convencoes de Dotfiles

## Origem Historica do "RC"

O sufixo **"rc"** origina-se do **CTSS (Compatible Time-Sharing System)** do MIT nos anos 1960. O sistema tinha uma funcionalidade chamada **"runcom"** (abreviacao de "run commands") que executava scripts de comandos armazenados em arquivos. Quando o Unix foi desenvolvido nos anos 1970 por Ken Thompson e Dennis Ritchie, eles adotaram essa convencao, nomeando o script de inicializacao do sistema como `/etc/rc`.

Existem duas interpretacoes aceitas:
- **Run Commands**: comandos a serem executados
- **Run Control**: controle de como programas se comportam durante a inicializacao

Fonte: [Baeldung - What Is rc and What Are rc Files?](https://www.baeldung.com/linux/rc-files)

---

## Comparacao Estruturada de Sistemas RC

### 1. Shell RC (.bashrc, .zshrc)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | Shell script (texto plano executavel) |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `.bashrc`, `.zshrc`, `.bash_profile`, `.zprofile` |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | `source arquivo` ou `. arquivo` |
| **Cascade** | Baseado em tipo de shell (login vs non-login) |
| **Heranca** | Manual via `source` - nao ha mecanismo automatico |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Completa (e um shell script) |

**Ordem de Carregamento (Bash):**
- Login shell: `.bash_profile` > `.bash_login` > `.profile`
- Non-login: `.bashrc`

**Zsh tem 5 arquivos de inicializacao:**
- `.zshenv` (sempre)
- `.zprofile` (login)
- `.zshrc` (interativo)
- `.zlogin` (login, apos .zshrc)
- `.zlogout` (logout)

Fonte: [Medium - Zsh/Bash startup files loading order](https://medium.com/@rajsek/zsh-bash-startup-files-loading-order-bashrc-zshrc-etc-e30045652f2e)

---

### 2. Git Configuration (.gitconfig)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | INI |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `.gitconfig`, `.git/config` |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | `[include]` e `[includeIf]` |
| **Cascade** | Sistema > Global (~/) > Local (.git/) > Worktree |
| **Heranca** | Condicional baseada em diretorio, branch, ou remote URL |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Limitada |

**Recurso Mais Sofisticado - `includeIf`:**
```ini
[includeIf "gitdir:~/work/"]
    path = ~/.gitconfig-work

[includeIf "gitdir:~/personal/"]
    path = ~/.gitconfig-personal

[includeIf "gitdir/i:~/Source/"]  # case-insensitive
    path = ~/.gitconfig-dev
```

**Condicoes suportadas:**
- `gitdir:` - baseado no caminho do repositorio
- `gitdir/i:` - case-insensitive
- `onbranch:` - baseado no branch atual
- `hasconfig:remote.*.url:` - baseado na URL do remote

Fonte: [Git Documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config), [CloudBees - .gitconfig's includeIf](https://www.cloudbees.com/blog/today-i-learned-gitconfigs-includeif)

---

### 3. TypeScript Configuration (tsconfig.json)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | JSON com comentarios (JSONC) |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `tsconfig.json`, `tsconfig.*.json` |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | `"extends": "..."` |
| **Cascade** | Base configs publicados via npm |
| **Heranca** | `compilerOptions` faz merge; `files/include/exclude` substitui |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Nao suportada |

**Exemplo de Heranca:**
```json
{
  "extends": "@tsconfig/node18/tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "./dist"
  }
}
```

**Comportamento importante:**
- `compilerOptions` sao **merged** (filho sobrescreve pai)
- `files`, `include`, `exclude` sao **substituidos** (nao merged)
- `references` **nunca** sao herdados
- Caminhos relativos resolvem a partir do arquivo de origem

Fonte: [TypeScript - TSConfig extends](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html)

---

### 4. ESLint Configuration (.eslintrc / eslint.config.js)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | JSON, YAML, JS (legacy); JS/TS (flat config) |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `.eslintrc.*` (deprecated), `eslint.config.js` (novo) |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | `extends` array |
| **Cascade** | Diretorio mais proximo tem prioridade (legacy) |
| **Heranca** | Plugins, rules, languageOptions podem ser herdados |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Via JS config |

**Sistema Legado (.eslintrc) - Cascade:**
```
projeto/
├── .eslintrc.json         # prioridade mais baixa
├── src/
│   └── .eslintrc.json     # sobrescreve o pai
└── tests/
    └── .eslintrc.json     # sobrescreve o pai
```
- Busca sobe na arvore de diretorios ate `root: true`

**Sistema Moderno (Flat Config) - Extends:**
```javascript
import js from "@eslint/js";
import tseslint from "typescript-eslint";

export default [
  js.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  {
    rules: {
      "no-console": "warn"
    }
  }
];
```

Fonte: [ESLint Configuration Files](https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/configuration-files)

---

### 5. EditorConfig (.editorconfig)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | INI-like |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `.editorconfig` |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | Nenhum - busca hierarquica automatica |
| **Cascade** | Arquivos mais proximos tem prioridade |
| **Heranca** | Automatica ate `root = true` |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Nao suportada |

**Comportamento:**
```ini
# ~/.editorconfig (global)
root = true

[*]
indent_style = space
indent_size = 4

# projeto/.editorconfig
root = false  # herda do global

[*.js]
indent_size = 2  # sobrescreve
```

- Busca comeca no arquivo sendo editado
- Sobe na arvore ate encontrar `root = true`
- Propriedades mais proximas vencem

Fonte: [EditorConfig.org](https://editorconfig.org/)

---

### 6. npm/Yarn Configuration (.npmrc, .yarnrc.yml)

| Aspecto | .npmrc | .yarnrc.yml (v2+) |
|---------|--------|-------------------|
| **Formato** | INI | YAML |
| **Cascade** | Projeto > Usuario > Global | Projeto > Usuario |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | Nenhum | Nenhum |
| **Heranca** | Automatica por localizacao | Automatica por localizacao |

**Localizacoes .npmrc:**
1. `/projeto/.npmrc` (projeto)
2. `~/.npmrc` (usuario)
3. `/usr/local/etc/npmrc` (global)
4. `/etc/npmrc` (sistema)

Fonte: [Yarn Configuration](https://yarnpkg.com/configuration/yarnrc)

---

### 7. Prettier (.prettierrc)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | JSON, YAML, JS, TOML |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `.prettierrc`, `.prettierrc.*`, `prettier.config.js` |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | **Nenhum nativo** |
| **Cascade** | Nenhum |
| **Heranca** | Apenas via JS com spread operator |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Apenas em JS config |

**Workaround para "extends":**
```javascript
// .prettierrc.mjs
import baseConfig from "@company/prettier-config";

export default {
  ...baseConfig,
  semi: false,
};
```

**Limitacao importante:** Prettier NAO tem `extends` nativo. Voce precisa usar JavaScript para herdar configuracoes.

Fonte: [Prettier - Sharing configurations](https://prettier.io/docs/sharing-configurations)

---

### 8. Babel (babel.config.js, .babelrc)

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| **Formato** | JSON, JS |
| **Convencao de Nome** | `babel.config.js`, `.babelrc`, `.babelrc.js` |
| **Mecanismo de Import** | `extends` |
| **Cascade** | `babel.config.json` < `.babelrc` < programmatic |
| **Heranca** | Presets e plugins tem logica especial de merge |
| **Expansao de Variaveis** | Via JS config |

**Ordem de plugins/presets:**
- Plugins: primeiro ao ultimo
- Presets: **ultimo ao primeiro** (invertido)

Fonte: [Babel - Configure Babel](https://babeljs.io/docs/configuration)

---

## Ranking de Sofisticacao

| Posicao | Sistema | Pontuacao | Justificativa |
|---------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **1** | **Git (.gitconfig)** | 10/10 | `includeIf` condicional (gitdir, branch, remote), include simples, merge automatico |
| **2** | **ESLint (flat config)** | 9/10 | Array composable, extends de plugins, overrides por glob |
| **3** | **TypeScript (tsconfig)** | 8/10 | extends via npm, merge inteligente de compilerOptions |
| **4** | **Shell (.bashrc/.zshrc)** | 7/10 | Flexibilidade total (e codigo), mas manual |
| **5** | **Babel** | 7/10 | extends, merge de presets, ordem invertida |
| **6** | **EditorConfig** | 6/10 | Cascade automatico elegante, mas sem import explicito |
| **7** | **npm/Yarn** | 5/10 | Cascade por localizacao, sem extends |
| **8** | **Prettier** | 3/10 | Sem extends nativo, requer JS workaround |

---

## Conclusao: Sistema Mais Sofisticado

O **Git (.gitconfig)** possui o sistema de configuracao RC mais sofisticado devido a:

1. **Includes Condicionais (`includeIf`)** - Pode carregar configs diferentes baseado em:
   - Caminho do repositorio (`gitdir:`)
   - Branch atual (`onbranch:`)
   - URL do remote (`hasconfig:remote.*.url:`)

2. **Cascade de 4 niveis**: Sistema > Global > Local > Worktree

3. **Glob patterns** em caminhos (`**/`, `/**`)

4. **Case-insensitive matching** (`gitdir/i:`)

Esse sistema permite cenarios complexos como usar email pessoal para projetos em `~/personal/` e email corporativo para `~/work/` automaticamente, sem nenhuma acao manual.

**Segundo lugar** vai para o **ESLint flat config** que, embora seja mais recente (2024-2025), oferece composabilidade via arrays JavaScript e extends sofisticado de plugins.

---

**Fontes:**
- [Baeldung - What Is rc and What Are rc Files?](https://www.baeldung.com/linux/rc-files)
- [Git Documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config)
- [TypeScript TSConfig extends](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html)
- [ESLint Configuration Files](https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/configuration-files)
- [EditorConfig.org](https://editorconfig.org/)
- [Prettier - Sharing configurations](https://prettier.io/docs/sharing-configurations)
- [Babel - Configure Babel](https://babeljs.io/docs/configuration)
- [Yarn Configuration](https://yarnpkg.com/configuration/yarnrc)
